---
title: "The Thermasight Model"
subtitle: "Predicting paragliding thermal triggers from LIDAR"
author: "James Maxwell"
date: today
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    papersize: a4
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - margin=2.4cm
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: "htbp"
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{microtype}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
header-includes:
  - \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
---

# What this model predicts {#sec-what}

Thermasight predicts **where on the ground a thermal will source and
trigger** — not where it will be once it is airborne. The output is a
raster (and a clustered point list) flagging the cells from which
buoyant air detaches from terrain and begins its climb.

Three physical conditions must hold *simultaneously* at a single
ground cell for it to be a trigger:

1. **Warm surface air has pooled there.** Boundary-layer air, heated
   by the terrain below, has been guided by topography to converge at
   this cell. Without a supply of warm air there is nothing to
   trigger.
2. **The cell has received enough solar energy.** Convergence alone
   is not sufficient. Two geometrically equivalent spurs facing
   different aspects have very different thermal strength depending
   on sun angle and time of day.
3. **The terrain is convex enough to cause detachment.** Rising warm
   air clings to a slope until the slope curves away beneath it. At
   a convex break — a cliff lip, a spur shoulder, a sudden
   steepening — the air can no longer follow the surface and
   detaches as a thermal bubble.

The model layers these three signals into a single trigger raster.
The rest of this document walks through each layer and shows what
it produces for the **Ullswater 10 km × 15 km** validation render
(NW wind at 10 km/h, 13:00 BST on 2026-05-10).

\newpage

# The hydrological analogy {#sec-hydro}

The engine rests on one analogy: **rising air on real terrain
follows the same gradient logic as falling water on inverted
terrain.** Given a digital elevation model $z(x, y)$ define the
inverted surface

$$
\tilde z(x, y) = \max(z) - z(x, y).
$$ {#eq-invert}

Standard hydrological flow accumulation applied to $\tilde z$
gives the **thermal convergence** field. Convex features on the
real terrain (spurs, cliff lips, shoulders) become concave on
$\tilde z$, so flow pools there — which is exactly where rising
warm boundary-layer air is predicted to converge.

In production the inversion is applied to a *Gaussian-smoothed,
wind-tilted* DEM (see @sec-wind). The smoothing kills sub-thermal
noise; the tilt biases the resulting convergence toward the lee
side of features, which is where surface warm air actually pools.

We use D∞ flow accumulation (Tarboton, 1997) via `richdem`. D8 is
too quantised for ridge-and-spur geometry; D∞ resolves the eight
sub-facets of every 3×3 stencil and partitions flow between two
downhill receivers in proportion to the geometry.

![The Ullswater DEM. Lake Ullswater (the centre-left dark band) is
flanked by the High Street range to the east and the Helvellyn /
Place Fell terrain to the west. Total relief ≈ 800 m.](figures/ullswater_compare_5m.png){#fig-dem width=100%}

\newpage

# Wind as terrain tilt {#sec-wind}

Wind in the surface boundary layer sweeps warm air downwind. Warm
air accumulates on the lee side of ridges and spurs and is
continually displaced from windward faces. The model captures this
by adding a linear ramp to the smoothed DEM *before inversion*:

$$
z_{\text{tilted}}(x, y) = z_{\text{smoothed}}(x, y)
  + k \, |\mathbf{u}| \bigl( x \sin\theta - y \cos\theta \bigr)
$$ {#eq-tilt}

where $\theta$ is the **wind-to** bearing in radians,
$|\mathbf{u}|$ is wind speed in m/s, and $k$ is an empirical
coefficient (default $0.03$ s/m). The ramp is highest in the
wind-to direction; after inversion that becomes the lowest part of
the inverted surface, so flow accumulates preferentially on the
lee side of every feature.

## Sign convention

| Wind from | Wind to    | Ramp peaks toward | Convergence biased toward |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| N (0°)    | S (180°)   | south             | south                      |
| S (180°)  | N (0°)     | north             | north                      |
| W (270°)  | E (90°)    | east              | east                       |
| SW (225°) | NE (45°)   | northeast         | northeast                  |

If the bias goes the wrong way, the ramp's sign is wrong. The
Ullswater render uses NW wind (from 315°, flowing toward SE / 135°),
so convergence biases toward the SE flanks of every ridge.

## What the tilt does *not* do

* It does not advect thermals after detachment — that is a separate,
  out-of-scope problem.
* It does not model rotors, lee-wave uplift, or wind shear.
* It does not capture valley-wind / synoptic-wind convergence
  lines.

These are documented limitations (see @sec-limits), not bugs.

\newpage

# Solar heating and land cover {#sec-heating}

Each cell injects buoyant energy into the air column above it at a
per-cell rate

$$
H(x, y, t) = \alpha(x, y) \bigl( s(x, y, t) \, I_{\text{beam}}(x, y, t)
                                  + I_{\text{diffuse}}(x, y, t) \bigr)
$$ {#eq-heating}

in W/m², where

* $I_{\text{beam}}$ and $I_{\text{diffuse}}$ are slope-projected
  direct and isotropic-diffuse clear-sky irradiance from `pvlib`
  (Ineichen-Perez model, Linke turbidity 3.0).
* $\alpha$ is surface absorptivity ($1 - \mathrm{albedo}$), either a
  scalar or a per-cell array driven by the UKCEH Land Cover Map.
* $s \in \{0, 1\}$ is a cast-shadow mask from a horizon scan along
  the solar azimuth. It multiplies only the beam term; diffuse
  irradiance comes from the whole sky and is not blocked by a
  single upwind ridge.

Slope and aspect are computed from the **raw** DEM via Horn (1981);
profile curvature uses Zevenbergen & Thorne (1987) on the same 3×3
stencil. Real geometry drives shadows and gradients — the heating
field never sees the smoothed or wind-tilted DEM.

@fig-alpha shows the Ullswater absorptivity field. Lake Ullswater
(α ≈ 0.05, near-total reflection plus evaporative loss) injects
effectively no heating, so the routing skips it entirely; this is a
load-bearing test case for the land-cover plumbing. Rugged
heather/grass uplands sit at α ≈ 0.75–0.85.

![Per-cell absorptivity α derived from the UKCEH Land Cover Map
2024 for the Ullswater tile. Lake water (deep navy) sits at
α ≈ 0.05; upland grass and heather sit at α ≈ 0.75–0.85.](figures/ullswater_alpha_5m.png){#fig-alpha width=85%}

\newpage

# The leaky-bucket kernel {#sec-leak}

The classical formulation routed heating as a weight on D∞ flow
accumulation and multiplied the result by positive curvature and a
slope mask. That has two physical defects: it double-counts energy
along the flow path (every cell sees the full upstream catchment in
its own accumulation value), and it has no mechanism for the
*cyclic-dump* regime that pilots observe on gentle terrain. The
production model replaces that step with a **leaky-bucket weighted
accumulation**.

Each cell $c$ has a steady-state through-flow rate $r(c)$ in
W/m²: the sum of its self-injection $H(c)$ and all upstream
contributions delivered along the inverted-and-tilted gradient.
The kernel routes $r(c)$ the same way as classical D∞ but at every
cell it splits the through-flow into a leak-out and a forward-on:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{leak}(c)     &= \bigl(1 - f_{\text{drain}}(c)\bigr) \, r(c) \\
\mathrm{forward}(c) &= f_{\text{drain}}(c) \, r(c)
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-leak-split}

Only $\mathrm{forward}(c)$ is dispatched to the two D∞ receivers;
$\mathrm{leak}(c)$ is consumed locally as **trigger output**.

## Drain fraction

The drain fraction depends on local geometry:

$$
f_{\text{drain}}(\kappa^+, \mathrm{slope}) = f_{\max}
  - (f_{\max} - f_{\min})
    \,\mathrm{sat}\!\left(\frac{\kappa^+}{\kappa_{\text{ref}}}\right)
    \,\mathrm{sat}\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{slope} - \theta_{\min}}{\theta_{\text{scale}}}\right)
$$ {#eq-fdrain}

with $\mathrm{sat}(x) = 1 - e^{-\max(x, 0)}$, the smooth
non-negative saturation. A sharp convex break with substantial
slope saturates both terms, driving $f_{\text{drain}}$ toward
$f_{\min}$ — the cell consumes most of its through-flow as trigger
output. A flat plateau leaves $f_{\text{drain}} = f_{\max} = 1$,
forwarding everything onward with nothing leaked.

Production defaults: $f_{\min} = 0.15$ (~15% slips past even the
sharpest break as boundary-layer skim), $f_{\max} = 1.0$,
$\kappa_{\text{ref}} = 0.005\ \mathrm{m^{-1}}$,
$\theta_{\min} = 2.5°$, $\theta_{\text{scale}} = 15°$.

## Energy conservation

Across the finite domain the kernel preserves total injected energy
exactly:

$$
\sum_c \mathrm{leak}(c) + \mathrm{residual\_at\_sinks} = \sum_c H(c)
$$ {#eq-conservation}

within float-precision rounding. This is the single tightest
correctness check on the topological pass. The Ullswater render
closes at 98.3 % leak / 1.7 % residual on a total of 3.32 × 10⁹ W.

![Absolute leak field on Ullswater. Bright cells are the high-rate
trigger sources; flat terrain and the lake stay dark. The
Mallerstang Edge–style scarp lines along the High Street range
(east of the lake) dominate, with the bowls and spur shoulders
filling in at lower intensity.](figures/ullswater_leak_5m.png){#fig-leak width=85%}

\newpage

# Cycle period {#sec-cycle}

The leaky kernel exposes a second per-cell field that the
predecessor formulation could not express. Each cell has a
buoyancy-storage capacity in J/m²:

$$
Q(\kappa^+, \mathrm{slope}) = Q_{\text{ref}} \,
  \exp\!\left(-\frac{\max(\kappa^+, 0)}{\kappa_{\text{ref}}}\right) \,
  \exp\!\left(-\frac{\max(\mathrm{slope} - \theta_{\min}, 0)}{\theta_{\text{scale}}}\right)
$$ {#eq-qstorage}

Storage is **large on flat / gentle terrain** (the boundary layer
can grow tall before the buoyancy cap is overcome) and **small on
sharp / steep terrain** (the geometry forces release at low
buildup). The cycle period at each cell is

$$
\tau(c) = \frac{Q(c)}{\mathrm{leak}(c)}, \qquad
+\infty \text{ where } \mathrm{leak}(c) = 0.
$$ {#eq-tau}

Interpretation: $r(c)$ is the time-averaged rate at which warm air
arrives at $c$; $\mathrm{leak}(c)$ is the time-averaged rate at
which the trigger releases energy; $\tau(c)$ is the period
between successive release events. A scarp lip with sharp
$\kappa^+$ has small $Q$ and large $\mathrm{leak}$, giving short
$\tau$ — the **consistent-trigger regime**. A gentle ridge has
large $Q$ and small $\mathrm{leak}$, giving long $\tau$ — the
**cyclic-dump regime**.

Both produce thermals, but the cycle period distinguishes
"reliable, frequent, small parcels" from "sporadic, big dumps",
which is pilot-actionable information.

![Buoyancy cycle period τ on Ullswater (log scale). Lighter cells
cycle quickly (~1 minute, scarp lips, the consistent-trigger
regime); darker cells cycle slowly (~10 minutes, gentle ridges
and broad spurs, the cyclic-dump regime). Transparent cells have
zero leak — the routing predicts no trigger
there.](figures/ullswater_cycle_5m.png){#fig-cycle width=85%}

\newpage

# Drafting / aggregation {#sec-draft}

Rising buoyant plumes coalesce as they ascend; a pilot at trigger
height (100–300 m AGL) samples a ground footprint several
thermal-column radii across. A diffuse spur leaking 1 W/m² over
$200 \times 200$ m injects the same total power as a scarp lip
leaking 400 W/m² over $10 \times 10$ m — and the spur's parcel is
typically the smoother climb.

A naïve "rank-normalise the cell-level leak and threshold at the
95th percentile" pipeline misses this: the spur's cells each sit at
a moderate rank and rarely clear the cut, while the scarp lip's
cells dominate. We therefore aggregate `leak` at a thermal-merging
scale before forming the trigger raster:

$$
\widetilde{\mathrm{leak}}(\mathbf{x})
  = G_{\sigma_{\text{draft}}} \star \mathrm{leak}(\mathbf{x})
  \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\bigl[\,\mathrm{slope}(\mathbf{x}) > \theta_{\min}\,\bigr]
$$ {#eq-draft}

where $G_{\sigma}$ is a NaN-aware Gaussian smoother with
$\sigma_{\text{draft}} = 75\ \mathrm{m}$ (roughly one thermal
column radius at low trigger altitude on UK upland terrain). The
slope mask is reapplied *after* the Gaussian so that bleed onto
flat plateaus does not reintroduce summit-interior brightness.

The Gaussian itself is sum-preserving on interior cells, so the
energy-conservation invariant of @eq-conservation on the underlying
$\mathrm{leak}$ field is untouched. The energy thrown away by the
post-smooth slope mask is logged as the scalar
`draft_mask_loss_total`; for Ullswater it was 2.63 % of total leak,
well within the budget.

![Aggregated draft potential on Ullswater. Compare against
@fig-leak: scarp lines are still visible but blurred; broad spur
shoulders and slope faces now show as coherent zones of moderate
intensity, where in the raw leak they appeared as faint
patches.](figures/ullswater_draft_5m.png){#fig-draft width=85%}

## The display raster — a rank-blend

A naïve $\mathrm{rank\_normalise}(\widetilde{\mathrm{leak}})$
display over-suppresses the cell-level scarp peaks that the
predecessor showed correctly: Gaussian aggregation dilutes a
concentrated peak by a factor of ~200 (at $\sigma_{\text{draft}}
= 75$ m on a 5 m grid) while leaving broad peaks roughly
untouched. The production trigger raster is therefore the per-cell
maximum of two independently rank-normalised fields:

$$
T(\mathbf{x}) = \max\!\bigl(
    \mathrm{rank\_normalise}(\mathrm{leak})(\mathbf{x}),\;
    \mathrm{rank\_normalise}(\widetilde{\mathrm{leak}})(\mathbf{x})
  \bigr).
$$ {#eq-trigger}

Ranking each field within its own positive-cell population puts
cells on equal footing: a scarp lip in the top 1 % of `leak` and a
spur shoulder in the top 1 % of `draft_potential` both land near
1.0, regardless of absolute W/m². The per-cell $\max$ then asks
"in which regime is this cell most extreme?" — preserving the
spur-rescue effect of drafting while keeping scarp visibility.

![Soaring view: trigger raster T with a $q_{0.80}$ floor over
hillshade. Cells below the floor render transparent so the terrain
reads through; the magma scale spans the top 20 % of trigger
cells. This is the panel that was compared against real flight
tracks on 2026-05-11.](figures/ullswater_trigger_5m.png){#fig-trigger width=85%}

\newpage

# End-to-end on Ullswater {#sec-overview}

@fig-overview is the six-panel anchor figure. Top row: input DEM,
the absorptivity field driving heating, and the soaring-view
trigger raster. Bottom row: the kernel's absolute leak output, the
post-aggregation draft potential, and the cycle-period raster.

![Ullswater six-panel overview. The figure suptitle includes the
canonical render parameters (date/time, wind, smoothing scales,
conservation closure, cluster count).](figures/ullswater_compare_5m.png){#fig-overview width=100%}

## Closure statistics

| Metric                    | Value             |
|---------------------------|-------------------|
| $\sum H$                  | $3.32 \times 10^9$ W |
| $\sum \mathrm{leak} / \sum H$ | 98.3 %        |
| Residual at sinks         | $5.49 \times 10^7$ W |
| Draft mask loss           | 2.63 % of leak    |
| Clusters at $q_{0.95}$    | 178               |
| Median cluster $\bar\tau$  | 62 s (consistent-trigger regime) |
| Pipeline runtime          | 23.6 s (3000 × 2000 cells, 5 m grid) |

The median cluster cycle of 62 s indicates a scarp-dominated trigger
field — exactly what you would expect over the Lake District's
glaciated valleys. Mallerstang's flatter limestone uplands give a
longer median (102 s on the analogous render); the contrast is a
real pilot-relevant signal that the cycle-period field surfaces.

\newpage

# First flight-log-backed validation {#sec-validation}

The Ullswater render was the **first real-flight validation** of the
production pipeline. The operator flew Ullswater on 2026-05-10 at
13:00 BST and compared the predicted trigger raster against the
climbs found on the day. A second pilot flew the same valley on the
same day.

## Flight one — operator

The operator flew SE across Ullswater toward the High Street range
and found climbs once away from the lake. The predicted top-8
clusters all sit east of Ullswater on the High Street massif and
adjacent fells:

| Rank | Mean strength (W/m²) | BNG E / N           | Feature                       |
|-----:|---------------------:|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1    | 3285                 | 343 394 / 522 320   | High Street / Loadpot Hill area |
| 2    | 2923                 | 337 100 / 522 718   | Bonscale Pike / Wether Hill   |
| 3    | 2895                 | 335 130 / 521 180   | Hartsop / Boredale Hause      |
| 4    | 2697                 | 335 302 / 522 873   | Head of Boredale              |
| 5    | 2601                 | 340 547 / 522 434   | Loadpot east face             |
| 6    | 2599                 | 340 097 / 523 500   | High Raise area               |
| 7    | 2346                 | 342 077 / 522 700   | Loadpot east face             |
| 8    | 2275                 | 338 510 / 523 266   | Heughscar / Askham Fell       |

Climbs encountered along the SE crossing toward High Street matched
the brightest cluster band in the predicted trigger raster.

## Flight two — one match, one out-of-scope miss

The second pilot flew east over the lake islands and **scratched
above W-facing slopes**, then eventually dropped into Boredale and
found a climb on the **southern back wall** — a feature the model
**did not predict**.

* The W-facing scratch is consistent with the model: W-facing
  slopes are *windward* under NW wind, so the wind-tilt mechanism
  (@eq-tilt) biases convergence away from them. The model correctly
  de-emphasised those features.
* The Boredale southern back-wall climb is a documented model
  limitation, not a bug. Valley-wind convergence lines and lee-wave
  uplift are not modelled (see @sec-limits). A valley-induced
  thermal release on a back wall is exactly the kind of feature
  only the planned WindNinja-driven phase could capture. The second
  pilot scratching for an extended period on the windward face
  before the convergence-line release triggered is consistent with
  that physics — valley-wind systems take time to set up.

## Headline

> "I didn't go searching for thermals; I just flew to where I
> thought they would be and got them in the locations indicated
> by our model."
>
> — operator, 2026-05-11

The model's predicted strong-cluster locations matched the climbs
found on the day. The single miss (Boredale back wall) is in the
documented out-of-scope territory of valley-wind convergence. No
in-scope failure observed.

\newpage

# Known limitations {#sec-limits}

The model is a static, gradient-only proxy for boundary-layer flow.
The following phenomena are **explicitly out of scope** and should
not be treated as bugs:

* **Valley-wind convergence lines.** Daytime thermal valley flows
  meeting synoptic wind release thermals at convergence boundaries
  that the static flow-accumulation analogy cannot see.
* **Lee-wave uplift.** Stratified flow over ridges produces standing
  waves whose lift bands are decoupled from the surface source map.
* **Mechanical rotors.** Strong-wind separation off lee faces
  produces turbulent rotors that are themselves thermal sources
  unrelated to surface heating.
* **Wind shear.** A single surface-layer wind vector misses
  directional shear with height.
* **Post-detachment drift.** The model finds the ground source;
  once airborne, parcels are advected by ambient flow, which is a
  separate problem (and one the model deliberately does not
  attempt).

The planned Phase 5 upgrade replaces the empirical wind-tilt
coefficient $k$ in @eq-tilt with a terrain-aware wind field
(WindNinja) and adds a Lagrangian plume model alongside the
hydrological analogy. WindNinja addresses valley-wind convergence
and lee-wave geometry directly; the plume model gives a second,
independent prediction of release density that can be cross-checked
against the leaky-kernel output.

\newpage

# Where to look next {#sec-next}

* **Phased build plan and validation gates** — `docs/ROADMAP.md`.
  The current phase is recorded at the top of that file.
* **Math and physics in full** — `docs/MODEL.md`. The leaky-bucket
  derivation is §11; earlier sections preserve the predecessor
  formulations as a historical record.
* **Validation log** — `docs/VALIDATION.md`. Every render that
  cleared a gate is documented there, including the Ullswater
  entry on 2026-05-11.
* **Data sources and absorptivity tables** — `docs/DATA.md`.
* **Auto-generated API reference** — `docs/api/` (run `make docs`
  to (re)build from the `thermal_model/` docstrings).
* **Outstanding follow-ups** — `docs/TODO.md`.

This PDF is regenerated by `bash docs/pdf/build.sh` whenever the
model or the Ullswater render changes. The build script reruns the
render if the figures are missing and then re-typesets via Quarto.
